mission.title: Install and Configure Dragonfly on Kubernetes
mission.description: Dragonfly is a cloud-native project that provides efficient, stable, and secure data distribution and acceleration using P2P technology. Installing Dragonfly allows you to improve the delivery of files, container images, and other artifacts in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Dragonfly Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Dragonfly Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add dragonfly https://dragonflyoss.github.io/dragonfly/charts
```

steps.title: Update Helm repositories
steps.description:
Update your local Helm chart repository cache to ensure you have the latest charts:
```bash
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Dragonfly using Helm
steps.description:
Install Dragonfly in a new namespace called 'dragonfly' with the specified version:
```bash
helm install dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly --version v2.4.1 --namespace dragonfly --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Dragonfly pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace dragonfly
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Dragonfly deployment to set resource limits for better performance:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment dragonfly --namespace dragonfly
```
Add the following under `spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Dragonfly will show the Dragonfly pods in the 'dragonfly' namespace as running. You should also have configured resource limits for optimal performance.
codeSnippets: helm install dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly --version v2.4.1 --namespace dragonfly --create-namespace

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Dragonfly Helm release from the cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall dragonfly --namespace dragonfly
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes associated with Dragonfly. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep dragonfly | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'dragonfly' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace dragonfly
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of Dragonfly. You can export the current configuration using:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace dragonfly -o yaml > dragonfly-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Dragonfly to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly --version v2.4.2 --namespace dragonfly
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Dragonfly pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace dragonfly
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see that the Dragonfly pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace dragonfly
``` 
Look for any error messages that might indicate the cause of the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Dragonfly service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and check the endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace dragonfly
kubectl describe svc dragonfly --namespace dragonfly
``` 
Look for any misconfigurations or issues with the endpoints.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource usage and limits:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace dragonfly
``` 
You may need to adjust the resource limits in the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that the release exists in the specified namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace dragonfly
``` 
If the release is missing, ensure that you are using the correct namespace and release name.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
